title: Quiz: Fidelity Benchmarks
description: Test your understanding of quantum hardware benchmarking methods and metrics

questions.question: In randomized benchmarking, the survival probability follows the form p_m = A * p^m + B. What does the parameter p represent?

options.text: The probability of measuring the correct state
The depolarizing parameter related to average gate fidelity
The state preparation fidelity
The measurement efficiency

questions.explanation: The parameter p is the depolarizing parameter, which characterizes how quickly the quantum state approaches the maximally mixed state as more gates are applied. The error per Clifford is r = (d-1)(1-p)/d, where d is the dimension. For single qubits, r = (1-p)/2.

questions.question: What is Quantum Volume (QV) = 2^6 = 64 telling us about a quantum computer?

options.text: The computer has exactly 64 qubits
The computer can reliably execute random SU(4) circuits of width and depth 6
The computer achieves 64% fidelity on benchmark circuits
The computer can factor 64-bit numbers

questions.explanation: Quantum Volume 2^n means the system can reliably execute random circuits with n qubits and depth n, achieving heavy output probability > 2/3. QV = 64 = 2^6 means circuits of width 6 and depth 6 can be executed with the required fidelity.

questions.question: CLOPS (Circuit Layer Operations Per Second) is important because it measures:

options.text: The maximum theoretical gate speed of the hardware
Real-world throughput including all classical and quantum overhead
The number of logical qubits available
The coherence time divided by gate time

questions.explanation: CLOPS measures actual circuit throughput in practice, including compilation time, job scheduling, classical-quantum communication, measurement, and parameter updates. This is critical for variational algorithms that require many circuit evaluations.

questions.question: In interleaved randomized benchmarking, how is a specific gate G's error rate determined?

options.text: By measuring the survival probability with only gate G applied repeatedly
By comparing the decay rates of standard RB and RB with G interleaved after each Clifford
By performing full process tomography on gate G
By measuring the gate G's effect on a Bell state

questions.explanation: Interleaved RB runs standard RB to get a reference decay rate p_ref, then runs RB with gate G inserted after each random Clifford to get p_G. The gate's error rate is extracted from the ratio p_G/p_ref, isolating G's contribution from the average Clifford error.

questions.question: The relationship between average gate fidelity F_avg and process fidelity F_process for a d-dimensional system is:

options.text: F_avg = F_process
F_avg = (d * F_process + 1) / (d + 1)
F_avg = F_process^2
F_avg = sqrt(F_process)

questions.explanation: Average gate fidelity and process fidelity are related by F_avg = (d*F_process + 1)/(d + 1). For single qubits (d=2), F_avg = (2*F_process + 1)/3. This means average fidelity is always closer to 1 than process fidelity.

questions.question: Which of the following are limitations of standard quantum volume as a benchmark? (Select all that apply)

options.text: It uses random circuits that may not reflect structured algorithm performance
It doesn't account for the speed of execution (throughput)
It can be optimized for specifically by cherry-picking the best qubits
It provides too much detail about individual gate errors

questions.explanation: QV limitations include: (a) random circuits may not predict performance on algorithms like VQE or QAOA, (b) QV doesn't measure speed - a system taking 1 minute has the same QV as one taking 1 hour for the same circuit, (c) vendors can select the best qubits for QV measurements. Option (d) is incorrect - QV provides a single aggregate number, not detailed gate-level information.

questions.question: For cross-entropy benchmarking (XEB), what does an XEB fidelity of F_XEB = 0.002 for a 53-qubit random circuit indicate?

options.text: The circuit failed completely with only 0.2% correct outputs
The output distribution correlates with the ideal distribution at a level far above random noise
The hardware has 0.2% gate fidelity
The measurement error rate is 99.8%

questions.explanation: XEB fidelity F_XEB = 2^n * <p_U(x)> - 1 measures correlation between measured and ideal distributions. For random outputs, F_XEB = 0. A value of 0.002 for 53 qubits means the quantum computer produces outputs correlated with the ideal distribution at ~0.2%, which is statistically significant and was the basis for Google's quantum supremacy claim.

questions.question: A quantum computer with higher Quantum Volume will always perform better on variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) calculations than one with lower Quantum Volume.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. QV measures performance on random circuits, which may not correlate with VQE performance. A system with lower QV but higher CLOPS might complete more VQE iterations per second, potentially finding better solutions faster. Additionally, VQE circuits have specific structure that differs from random QV circuits, so gate fidelities on the relevant gates matter more than the aggregate QV metric.
